1: tessaras anemous — the four winds from the four corners; Zech 6:5 (the four spirits/winds of heaven) and Dan 7:2 (the four winds stirring up the sea from which the beasts arise). The holding back of the four winds is a temporary restraint on cosmic judgment — the angels hold history in suspension before the sealing.
2: sphragida Theou zōntos — the seal of the living God. Sphragis (seal/signet) marks ownership and guarantees authenticity; in Ezek 9:4, YHWH marks the foreheads of those who mourn over Jerusalem before the destroying angels go through. Rev 7 is the eschatological counterpart: God's own mark of ownership protects from the coming judgment. anatolē hēliou (rising of the sun, the east) — the direction associated with the returning glory of God (Ezek 43:2; 44:1-2).
3: sphragisōmen (aorist subjunctive: until we might seal) — the sealing is a punctiliar act to be completed before harm is permitted. The aorist subjunctive of purpose in a temporal clause stresses the completion of the sealing before the wind-release. epi tōn metōpōn autōn — on their foreheads — Exod 13:9 places the Passover sign as a reminder "on your forehead"; Rev places the ownership-seal on the same location, linking Passover protection with the new-Exodus sealing.
4: hekaton tesserakonta tessares chiliades (144,000) — the number is 12 × 12 × 1,000: the arithmetic of symbolic completeness. Twelve is the number of the tribes of Israel (the covenant people); twelve squared (the people in their fullness) multiplied by a thousand (the number of divine abundance). The tribal list that follows (vv5-8) deviates from standard OT lists in significant ways — Judah is first (not Reuben the firstborn); Dan is omitted; Manasseh replaces it — suggesting symbolic rather than literal enumeration.
5: Judah is listed first (not Reuben, the firstborn), because the Lamb who opens the seals is from Judah (5:5). The tribal list is reordered around the Lion of Judah: the tribe that produced the Messiah heads the eschatological register. The twelve-thousand-per-tribe structure is perfectly uniform — no tribe has more or fewer — signaling an idealized, symbolic completeness.
6: The tribal enumeration (vv5-8) proceeds through the list with mechanical regularity. Dan is absent and Manasseh is added (both son and father of Joseph appear). Early patristic tradition suggested Dan was omitted because Antichrist would come from Dan (Gen 49:17 — "Dan will be a serpent by the roadside"); whether that is the author's intent is uncertain. The completeness of twelve tribes is maintained by the substitution.
7: Simeon and Levi — the violent brothers of Gen 34 who were scattered (Gen 49:5-7) — appear in the sealing list. Jacob's curse on them is not the final word: the eschatological community includes even those whose ancestors were under judgment. The tribal list is redemptive rather than merely genealogical.
8: The list closes with Benjamin. The twelve tribes together constitute the new Israel — not a literal census but a symbolic statement of the complete covenantal community that will survive the judgment. The sealing answers the question of v17 ("who is able to stand?"): those on whom God's own seal has been placed.
9: ochlus polys hon arithmēsai auton oudeis ēdynato — "a great multitude which no one was able to count." This directly contrasts with the 144,000 who were precisely numbered; the two visions are likely the same community seen from different angles — the finite covenant-structure (144,000) and the infinite eschatological reality (innumerable multitude). stolas leukas (white robes) and phoinikes (palm branches) — the palm branches recall the Feast of Booths (Lev 23:40; Neh 8:15) and the triumphal entry (John 12:13), both moments of covenant celebration.
10: hē sōtēria tō Theō hēmōn — "salvation belongs to our God." The dative of possession: salvation is not a human achievement but the property of God, who loans it to his people. The parallel "and to the Lamb" makes salvation jointly attributed to God and the Lamb — the same co-attribution seen throughout the doxologies of Revelation (cf. 5:13).
11: epesan epi ta prosōpa autōn (fell on their faces) — the standard LXX gesture of prostration before the divine Presence (Ezek 1:28; 3:23; 43:3; 44:4). In Revelation, the twenty-four elders fall prostrate repeatedly (4:10; 5:8,14; 7:11; 11:16; 19:4). The accumulated weight of repetition establishes the posture of creation before the Creator as the fundamental mode of heavenly existence.
12: The sevenfold doxology (eulogia, doxa, sophia, eucharistia, timē, dynamis, ischys: blessing, glory, wisdom, thanksgiving, honor, power, might) is framed by matching Amēn. The seven attributes in doxological form recall the throne-room hymns of chs 4-5. The addition of eucharistia (thanksgiving) to the list distinguishes this doxology — the redeemed multitude adds gratitude to the creation's praise.
13: apokritheis ... eipen — one of the elders takes the role of interpretive guide, addressing John with a rhetorical question designed to draw out the answer the elder will then supply. This Socratic pedagogical technique (the leader asks, the pupil professes ignorance, the leader explains) is found in apocalyptic literature (Dan 7:16; 4 Ezra 4:52-5:13). John's "Sir, you know" (Kyrie mou, sy oidas) is the appropriate deferential response.
14: hoi erchomenoi ek tēs thlipseōs tēs megalēs — "those coming out of the great tribulation" — present participle: they are in the process of emerging, not yet finished. eplynan tas stolas autōn (aorist: they washed their robes) and eleukanan (aorist: made them white) — both completed acts. The paradox: made white in blood. Haimati (blood, dative of means/instrument) — the blood of the Lamb is the cleansing agent that reverses the normal function of blood (which stains). The syntax forces the paradox: "they made them white in blood."
15: latreuousin autō hēmeras kai nyktos — "they serve him day and night" — the eschatological reversal of the present situation (where God's servants are persecuted and killed). Latreuō is the technical term for priestly/liturgical service (Heb 9:9; 10:2; 13:10). The redeemed serve as priests in the heavenly temple. skēnōsei ep' autous — "will spread his tent over them" (future); the verb skēnoō echoes both the wilderness Tabernacle and John 1:14 (the Word tabernacled among us); here it is the permanent eschatological dwelling.
16: ou peinasousin eti oude dipsēsousin eti — future tenses (both negated by eti, "any longer/still") describing permanent reversal. The "no longer" structure signals that the present experience is hunger and thirst — these are martyrs who suffered real deprivation. The promise reverses their specific earthly experience. ou mē pesē ep' autous ho hēlios — Isa 49:10 ("they will not hunger or thirst; neither the scorching wind nor the sun will beat on them") is quoted almost verbatim; the servant's promise is fulfilled in the great multitude.
17: to arnion ... poimanei autous — the Lamb will shepherd them (future poimainō). The word for shepherd the flock is applied to the Lamb — the most compressed theological paradox in the chapter: the sacrificed animal is the shepherd. Behind this stands Ezek 34:23 (David my servant will shepherd them) and Ps 23 (YHWH my shepherd). zōēs (of life) modifying "springs" (not "rivers of life" but pēgas hydatōn zōēs, springs of living water) — echoes Jer 2:13 (YHWH as the spring of living water) and John 4:14; 7:38. exaleipsei ... pan dakryon (will wipe away every tear) — Isa 25:8; the eschatological comfort of YHWH personally wiping tears.
